coverage.note: covered = a cross-vendor-matched producer derivation that is input-grounded, non-trivial and self-consistent; text-grounded also matches a number in the prose.

faithful_catches.note: worksheet slips are the extraction-free catch: producer expr != producer's own stated result. Zero here means the finals' load-bearing arithmetic is internally sound.

self_extraction_noise.note: asking the producer to ALSO self-report 'what the prose asserts' re-introduces the Step-3 extraction bottleneck: this many sound derivations carry a wrong self-report. The verdict deliberately does NOT gate on that field.

head_to_head_vs_audit.audit_desc: Price increase to $11/mo represents +18% ARPU over $9/mo base

derivations.desc: Option (b) time-to-live must land inside the 12-month agency deadline; the ~4 month go-live plus diligence sprint provides the slack that makes (b) viable within the clock, whereas (a)'s timeline does not fit.
Runway constraint disqualifying (a): the company has 14 months of runway but (a) takes 12-18 months and diverts capital, so its timeline exceeds runway, versus the 12-month deadline it must meet.
Net revenue retained under (b) after the 25% revenue share, which must be gross-margin-positive; drives the unit-economics gate.
Cost of (a) as a fraction of the annual deal value, illustrating why building directly is prohibitive relative to the runway-constrained revenue it would fund.

Corrected FTE requirement for sustainable 24/7/365 overnight-only coverage; load-bearing because the recommendation explicitly rejects the 3-FTE figure and raises the feasibility bar based on a higher FTE count. Nights per year divided by realistic annual night-shifts per FTE after leave/CME/gaps (assumption: ~55 nights/FTE/yr sustainable).
Annual cost of the incumbent flat 22% renewal, a baseline the decision rule compares against carve-out cost.
Annual cost of premium 20-min SLA tier (+31%), one of the options weighed in the fallback decision rule.
Stroke/trauma carve-out volume that (c) brings into dedicated-capacity fast lane; load-bearing because carve-out cost/feasibility depends on this subset size.
Full in-house cost at corrected 6-7+ FTE, the cost bar that the recommendation says routes the fallback toward a vendor carve-out rather than DIY in-house.
Battery net capex after 30% federal ITC — load-bearing for the budget floor and for anchoring on the battery despite high capex
DR firm low-end MW at enforceable realized-curtailment fraction — used to justify crediting DR only at its low end (~17-19 MW)
DR firm lower-bound MW after opt-out erosion below the 55% floor — supports the ~17 MW end of the credited range
Battery capacity degradation over the year-3 need horizon — affects whether 40 MW nameplate still meets the accredited need
Reserve gap in percentage points that must be closed to meet the 12% policy floor; the decision rests on this being a binding additive requirement on top of the gap.

Dollar size of the reserve shortfall the supplemental increment must cover, estimated as ~1.6 points of expenditures; used to justify that no enumerated package closes gap+reserve alone and a supplemental is needed.
Margin of Option (a) over the required gap, showing how much cushion (a) has if the ambulance fee is haircut; supports the claim that fleet deferral can backfill.
Total cost of an unexpected trip (lost margin plus disposal), which the seal-kit expedite decision is weighed against as insurance
Cost of the expedited seal kit, compared against the trip cost to justify ordering it as insurance regardless of scope
Option (c) total direct cost: incremental per-order fees on 40% of forecast volume over the peak period plus onboarding, used to show (c) exceeds the $120K budget cap
Incremental per-order fee portion of (c) alone, to establish the ~$90.7K component
In-house residual volume (c) leaves the pack line to handle (60% of forecast), which determines whether the binding SLA constraint is still exposed under (c)
Option (b) labor cost, full 30-temp configuration over 8 weeks at 16 hrs/temp weekly, plus training/agency fees — establishes (b) stays in budget
Option (b) reduced labor config (24 temps + OT), used as the fallback cost when weekday capacity is sufficient
Net tuition revenue after enrollment-decline haircut; determines how much of the $4.2M gap the tuition lever verifiably covers
Residual gap remaining after verified-conservative tuition net, which must be closed by vacant-staff eliminations
Advising caseload increase under option (a) that threatens retention target
Retention gap that must be closed to avoid performance-funding penalty; drives prioritizing retention protection
Baseline current forage cost per month under existing contract (6,000 tons at $245/ton), used as the reference point for comparing option costs.
Option (a) cost per month: take-or-pay for 6,000 tons at $310/ton. This is the fallback option cost.
Option (c) forage cost per month: 1,800 tons corn silage at $92/ton plus 700 tons soy hull pellets at $265/ton. This is the cost basis for the recommended option.
Monthly cost advantage of option (c) versus option (a): the ~$650k/month savings figure that drives the decision to prefer (c).
The nominal price-tier delta cost of option (c): 15% of 2.5M gallons downgraded at $0.12/gallon less, the quality-tail cost the Challenger claims understates the true risk.
Penalty exposure on fixed retail contracts if milk output drops: $0.18/gallon on 1.2M gallons/month, part of the tail-risk quantification for grade-linked contracts.
Annual plan pulls forward 12 months of cash per subscriber at the discounted annual price, which is the core cash-improvement mechanism that makes (b) win in its cell
Discounted annual price implied by the recommended alternative discount range (10-15%) applied to 12 months at $9/mo, showing the safeguard's suggested pricing rather than the stated 20% discount
Option (a) new monthly price that raises collected ARPU without creating deferred-revenue liability; load-bearing for the (a) routing
The prior price-increase cancellation spike used to model (a)'s downside risk
Forecast churn under (a) used to model the downside of the fallback option
Conversion-drop revert threshold for (b)'s conversion guard, taken from the stated 8% friction figure
Contractor cost that makes (c) inferior on headcount/cash grounds
Gross runway in months before any trial-specific spend, used to show (b) cannot be launched and operated on cash and that the fallback must switch to (c)
Gap between (b)'s cost and available cash, showing (b) cannot be funded without incremental capital that by assumption does not exist
Whether (c)'s cost fits within available cash, making it the only executable/survivable path under the binding constraint
OTP improvement required from baseline to target — the binding KPI the recommendation says only package (a) can structurally meet
Total pilot budget cap that scope must be locked to, combining capex and ops for package (a)
Time buffer between the treaty bind-decision cutoff and the effective date, establishing the window available to resolve subjectivities before inception
Rate-on-limit for option (a), the hazard-priced choice, used to justify pricing adequacy versus (b)
Rate-on-limit for option (b), shown to be materially lower than (a) despite full limit and a live dominant peril, supporting the claim that (b) is underpriced
